title: Early JAL redirect in ID stage — save 1 bubble per unconditional jump
motivation:
CoreMark is function-call heavy (matrix_test, core_state_transition,
core_list_find, crcu8, list_*, etc. all invoke subroutines). Every JAL
currently costs TWO pipeline bubbles because the jump is resolved in
EX (flushing IF and ID). But JAL is fully decodable at the ID stage:
the target is `pc + J-immediate`, with no register dependency and no
condition to evaluate. Resolving it one stage earlier shaves one
bubble per call — measurable on a call-heavy workload — without
touching the Fmax-critical ALU path, the multiplier, or the
forwarding network. Current Fmax (135.21 MHz) is already at target,
so an IPC-only improvement translates directly to fitness gain.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark is function-call heavy (matrix_test, core_state_transition,
core_list_find, crcu8, list_*, etc. all invoke subroutines). Every JAL
currently costs TWO pipeline bubbles because the jump is resolved in
EX (flushing IF and ID). But JAL is fully decodable at the ID stage:
the target is `pc + J-immediate`, with no register dependency and no
condition to evaluate. Resolving it one stage earlier shaves one
bubble per call — measurable on a call-heavy workload — without
touching the Fmax-critical ALU path, the multiplier, or the
forwarding network. Current Fmax (135.21 MHz) is already at target,
so an IPC-only improvement translates directly to fitness gain.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an ID-stage JAL early-redirect path. Concretely:

1. In id_stage, compute `jal_target = pc + J-imm` combinationally
   whenever the decoded instruction is a JAL (is_jump && !is_jalr &&
   !is_illegal && valid && no flush). Drive a new `redirect_id` /
   `redirect_id_target` pair up to if_stage and the hazard unit.
2. In if_stage, give EX redirect priority over ID redirect (EX's
   resolved branch/JALR is the ground-truth mispredict path), and
   give ID redirect priority over the default pc+4.
3. In hazard_unit, a new `redirect_id` signal triggers `flush_if`
   (NOP into the IF/ID bundle) — flushing ONE stage instead of two
   since the JAL itself is now in ID and will propagate normally to
   EX/MEM/WB so the rd=PC+4 writeback still happens.
4. Add a `predicted` bit to id_ex_t and ex_mem_t. When ID issues
   early redirect, the JAL propagates with `predicted=1`. In
   ex_stage, the EX redirect fires only when either (a) the
   instruction is NOT predicted, or (b) the predicted target
   disagrees with the EX-resolved target (defensive — should never
   trigger for JAL since both use pc+J-imm). This keeps JALR and
   BRANCH flushes unchanged (their `predicted=0` stays).
5. JAL writeback (rd=PC+4), misaligned-jump trap detection, and
   RVFI pc_wdata semantics remain exactly where they are today (in
   EX stage); invariants 2/4/5 unaffected because the retirement
   stream and trap discipline don't move.
6. On EX-stage misprediction (which shouldn't happen for JAL but
   the defensive check covers it), fall back to full 2-stage flush
   exactly like today — no regression path weakening.

Expected effect: every JAL saves exactly one cycle. CoreMark's
inner loops contain ~several JALs per iteration through function
calls; empirically 2–5% IPC improvement is realistic. The added
logic is tiny (one 32-bit adder + one mux in ID, one extra bit on
pipeline registers), well off the ALU/multiplier critical path, so
Fmax should hold at ~135 MHz. No decoder changes — the existing
JAL recognition feeds the new logic unchanged.

changes.description: Add `predicted` bit to id_ex_t and ex_mem_t pipeline bundles so the EX stage can distinguish JALs already redirected by ID from fresh control flow.

Compute jal_target = pc + J-imm combinationally for JAL (is_jump && !is_jalr). Emit redirect_id + redirect_id_target to core.sv. Latch ctrl.predicted=1 into the ID/EX register when early redirect fires.

Accept a second redirect input (redirect_id, redirect_id_target). Priority: EX redirect > ID redirect > pc+4. Emit NOP on either redirect kind (existing flush path already does this via if_stage's flush input).

Suppress redirect when the in-flight JAL arrives with predicted=1 AND its EX-computed target matches the predicted target. Leave JALR/BRANCH redirect paths unchanged. Propagate predicted into ex_mem_t (defensive; not consumed downstream but keeps the bundle consistent for waveform debug).
Add redirect_id input; on redirect_id without EX redirect, assert flush_if only (ID captures the JAL normally). Keep the full 2-stage flush behavior on EX redirect (branch mispredict / JALR).
Wire redirect_id / redirect_id_target between id_stage, if_stage, and hazard_unit. No RVFI port changes; channel-1 remains tied off; retirement semantics unchanged.

Add a cocotb test that fires a sequence of JALs and checks that the observed cycle count between retirements is reduced by one per JAL versus the baseline, confirming the early-redirect path is active.
Add `predicted` bit to id_ex_t and ex_mem_t pipeline bundles so the EX stage can distinguish JALs already redirected by ID from fresh control flow.

Compute jal_target = pc + J-imm combinationally for JAL (is_jump && !is_jalr). Emit redirect_id + redirect_id_target to core.sv. Latch ctrl.predicted=1 into the ID/EX register when early redirect fires.

Accept a second redirect input (redirect_id, redirect_id_target). Priority: EX redirect > ID redirect > pc+4. Emit NOP on either redirect kind (existing flush path already does this via if_stage's flush input).

Suppress redirect when the in-flight JAL arrives with predicted=1 AND its EX-computed target matches the predicted target. Leave JALR/BRANCH redirect paths unchanged. Propagate predicted into ex_mem_t (defensive; not consumed downstream but keeps the bundle consistent for waveform debug).
Add redirect_id input; on redirect_id without EX redirect, assert flush_if only (ID captures the JAL normally). Keep the full 2-stage flush behavior on EX redirect (branch mispredict / JALR).
Wire redirect_id / redirect_id_target between id_stage, if_stage, and hazard_unit. No RVFI port changes; channel-1 remains tied off; retirement semantics unchanged.

Add a cocotb test that fires a sequence of JALs and checks that the observed cycle count between retirements is reduced by one per JAL versus the baseline, confirming the early-redirect path is active.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r1s0 (regression, +1.67%): ID-stage JAL early-redirect adds a decode→J-imm-add→IF-PC-mux combinational path plus a new priority tier (EX>ID>pc+4) on the Fmax-critical fetch PC selection; the per-JAL bubble savings on CoreMark's call-heavy inner loops don't offset the Fmax/area cost, so "IPC-only" optimizations on the PC path are rarely free.

title: Extract DIV/REM into iterative multi-cycle unit to shorten ALU critical path
motivation:
The current ALU is fully combinational — every op, including 32-bit signed/unsigned
division and remainder, is computed in a single EX-stage cycle via SystemVerilog
`/` and `%`. On the Tang Nano 20K (Gowin GW2AR-18) there are no DSP blocks that
map division; synthesis emits a restoring-divider tree that is almost certainly
BOTH the timing-critical path AND a significant LUT4 consumer.

Current fitness is Fmax-dominated: 135.21 MHz on a 5-stage in-order pipeline is
low for a design this simple. Removing the combinational divider should let the
EX stage re-close timing around just the adder/shifter/logic/comparator and
DSP-backed multiplier, freeing considerable slack.

CoreMark performs very few DIV/REM ops (the hot loops are integer add/shift/
compare/MUL and memcpy-like stores). Paying 32 cycles for a rare DIV is a
great trade when every other instruction gets a shorter clock period.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current ALU is fully combinational — every op, including 32-bit signed/unsigned
division and remainder, is computed in a single EX-stage cycle via SystemVerilog
`/` and `%`. On the Tang Nano 20K (Gowin GW2AR-18) there are no DSP blocks that
map division; synthesis emits a restoring-divider tree that is almost certainly
BOTH the timing-critical path AND a significant LUT4 consumer.

Current fitness is Fmax-dominated: 135.21 MHz on a 5-stage in-order pipeline is
low for a design this simple. Removing the combinational divider should let the
EX stage re-close timing around just the adder/shifter/logic/comparator and
DSP-backed multiplier, freeing considerable slack.

CoreMark performs very few DIV/REM ops (the hot loops are integer add/shift/
compare/MUL and memcpy-like stores). Paying 32 cycles for a rare DIV is a
great trade when every other instruction gets a shorter clock period.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Pull ALU_DIV / ALU_DIVU / ALU_REM / ALU_REMU out of the combinational `alu`
module and into a new `div_unit.sv` iterative restoring divider:
  - 32-cycle unsigned restoring-divide datapath (33-bit partial remainder,
    1 subtract + 1 shift per cycle) with a tiny pre/post sign-fixup wrapper
    for signed DIV/REM per RV32M semantics.
  - 2-state FSM (IDLE, BUSY) with a `busy` output that drives a new
    `div_stall` input on `hazard_unit`. While busy: stall_id + stall_ex_mem
    hold the ID/EX and EX/MEM registers, flush_if emits bubbles so no new
    work enters, hold_mem_wb keeps MEM/WB quiet so rvfi_order doesn't jump.
  - On completion (cycle 32), the result is muxed into the EX-stage ALU
    output path for one cycle, EX/MEM captures it normally, `busy` drops,
    and the pipeline resumes.
  - Preserve the existing RV32M edge-case semantics (DIV-by-0 → -1,
    INT_MIN/-1 overflow → INT_MIN, REM-by-0 → dividend, etc.) in the
    sign-fixup wrapper; same table as current `alu.sv`.
  - Keep the `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` ifdef path IDENTICAL (the cheap XOR
    stand-ins stay in `alu.sv` and complete in one cycle; the iterative
    unit is only instantiated in the non-ALTOPS build). This preserves
    formal-gate runtime on every iteration.
  - MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU stay combinational in `alu.sv` — they map cleanly
    to Gowin DSP blocks and aren't the Fmax bottleneck.

Expected effect:
  + Fmax: shorter EX combinational cone, likely +15..25% (critical path
    currently threads the ~32-level divider tree). This dominates fitness.
  + LUT4: iterative divider uses ~200 LUTs vs. the ~1000+ LUT
    combinational tree → -5..-10% area, freeing headroom.
  - IPC: CoreMark 10-iter run executes a handful of DIV/REM at most
    (mostly in printf, outside the timing markers). Even 100 DIVs × 32
    cycles = 3200 cycles over ~4.5M total = <0.1% IPC loss.

Net: fitness = IPC × Fmax should rise meaningfully because the Fmax gain
vastly exceeds the IPC cost.

changes.description: New iterative 32-cycle restoring divider. Inputs: start, op (DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU), a, b. Outputs: busy, done, result. 2-state FSM; pre-sign-fixup on a,b; post-sign-fixup on result; handle div-by-0 and INT_MIN/-1 overflow per RV32M semantics in 1 cycle fast-path.

Remove DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU from the combinational case (keep the ALTOPS branch unchanged). MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU stay combinational. Add a defensive default so removed ops don't silently return 0 during EX — the div_unit drives the final result via ex_stage mux.

Instantiate div_unit. On ID/EX.ctrl.alu_op ∈ {DIV,DIVU,REM,REMU} fire start for one cycle, assert div_stall until busy drops, mux div_unit.result into the EX/MEM.alu_result write on the completion cycle. Preserve misalign_fault / redirect logic.

Add div_stall input from ex_stage. Fold into stall_if / stall_id / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb the same way dmem_stall is handled (priority: redirect still wins for branch resolution, but a DIV in flight must complete before the redirect is honoured — use the existing stall_ex_mem pathway).
Wire new div_stall signal from ex_stage to hazard_unit. No IO-contract changes: the top-level core port set (imem/dmem/RVFI_0/RVFI_1) is untouched.

Update tests: DIV/REM vectors now need to drive `start` and wait for `done` on the new div_unit, rather than reading the combinational alu output. Keep the same 30+ vectors per op covering signed/unsigned/by-zero/overflow edge cases to preserve invariant 5b.
Add a regression test: a sequence of DIV followed by dependent ADD must stall EX for ~32 cycles then retire in order with correct rd_wdata; RVFI order monotonic; no trap on legal DIV.

New iterative 32-cycle restoring divider. Inputs: start, op (DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU), a, b. Outputs: busy, done, result. 2-state FSM; pre-sign-fixup on a,b; post-sign-fixup on result; handle div-by-0 and INT_MIN/-1 overflow per RV32M semantics in 1 cycle fast-path.

Remove DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU from the combinational case (keep the ALTOPS branch unchanged). MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU stay combinational. Add a defensive default so removed ops don't silently return 0 during EX — the div_unit drives the final result via ex_stage mux.

Instantiate div_unit. On ID/EX.ctrl.alu_op ∈ {DIV,DIVU,REM,REMU} fire start for one cycle, assert div_stall until busy drops, mux div_unit.result into the EX/MEM.alu_result write on the completion cycle. Preserve misalign_fault / redirect logic.

Add div_stall input from ex_stage. Fold into stall_if / stall_id / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb the same way dmem_stall is handled (priority: redirect still wins for branch resolution, but a DIV in flight must complete before the redirect is honoured — use the existing stall_ex_mem pathway).
Wire new div_stall signal from ex_stage to hazard_unit. No IO-contract changes: the top-level core port set (imem/dmem/RVFI_0/RVFI_1) is untouched.

Update tests: DIV/REM vectors now need to drive `start` and wait for `done` on the new div_unit, rather than reading the combinational alu output. Keep the same 30+ vectors per op covering signed/unsigned/by-zero/overflow edge cases to preserve invariant 5b.
Add a regression test: a sequence of DIV followed by dependent ADD must stall EX for ~32 cycles then retire in order with correct rd_wdata; RVFI order monotonic; no trap on legal DIV.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-002-r1s1 (improvement, +13.29%): Combinational `/` and `%` synthesize to a ~32-level restoring-divider tree that dominates both EX critical path and LUT4 area on DSP-less Gowin parts, and since CoreMark hits DIV/REM a handful of times (mostly outside timing markers), pulling them into an iterative 32-cycle `div_unit` with hazard-unit stall is a near-pure Fmax/area win — always keep the ALTOPS branch identical in `alu.sv` so formal BMC stays tractable.

title: IF-stage BTB + bimodal predictor to eliminate taken-branch bubbles
motivation:
Today the 5-stage in-order pipeline resolves every control transfer in
EX. A taken branch or jump flushes two in-flight instructions (the one
in IF->ID this cycle, plus the one captured into ID/EX next cycle), so
every taken CTI costs a fixed 2-cycle bubble. Untaken conditional
branches cost 0 because the static predict-not-taken path matches the
pc+4 default fetch.

CoreMark is branch-dense: the inner loops of matrix_mul_matrix_bitextract,
core_list_mergesort, crc16, and core_bench_state all spin on backward
branches that are taken the overwhelming majority of the time, plus
every function call/return is a JAL + JALR pair that unconditionally
costs the 2-cycle bubble today. Back-of-the-envelope: if taken CTIs
are ~15% of retired instructions (typical for integer workloads) and
we pay 2 bubble cycles each, that is ~30% CPI overhead attributable
to branches alone. Even a modest-accuracy predictor that covers the
hot loops and function returns should claw back a large chunk of that.

There is headroom in the LUT budget (9880/10000) but it is tight, and
Fmax is already at 135 MHz. The predictor must be cheap and must not
sit on the IF critical path.

hypothesis.motivation:
Today the 5-stage in-order pipeline resolves every control transfer in
EX. A taken branch or jump flushes two in-flight instructions (the one
in IF->ID this cycle, plus the one captured into ID/EX next cycle), so
every taken CTI costs a fixed 2-cycle bubble. Untaken conditional
branches cost 0 because the static predict-not-taken path matches the
pc+4 default fetch.

CoreMark is branch-dense: the inner loops of matrix_mul_matrix_bitextract,
core_list_mergesort, crc16, and core_bench_state all spin on backward
branches that are taken the overwhelming majority of the time, plus
every function call/return is a JAL + JALR pair that unconditionally
costs the 2-cycle bubble today. Back-of-the-envelope: if taken CTIs
are ~15% of retired instructions (typical for integer workloads) and
we pay 2 bubble cycles each, that is ~30% CPI overhead attributable
to branches alone. Even a modest-accuracy predictor that covers the
hot loops and function returns should claw back a large chunk of that.

There is headroom in the LUT budget (9880/10000) but it is tight, and
Fmax is already at 135 MHz. The predictor must be cheap and must not
sit on the IF critical path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small branch target buffer with a bimodal direction predictor,
looked up combinationally in IF alongside the imem fetch. Concretely:

1. rtl/bpu.sv (new): 32-entry direct-mapped BTB indexed by PC[6:2].
   Each entry holds {valid, tag[24:0], target[31:0], ctr[1:0]}. Predict
   taken when the entry is valid, tag matches, and ctr[1]=1. JAL/JALR
   entries are forced to ctr=2'b11 on allocation (unconditional-taken).
   The predicted-PC path is: pc_next = hit_and_taken ? btb_target : pc+4.
   Lookup is a single LUT-RAM read plus a 25-bit comparator — short
   enough to coexist with the existing imem-addr fanout inside one
   135 MHz cycle on GW2AR-18.

2. rtl/if_stage.sv: consume the BPU's (predict_taken, predict_target,
   predict_valid) and forward them down the pipeline inside if_id_t /
   id_ex_t / ex_mem_t so EX can detect misprediction.

3. rtl/core_pkg.sv: extend the pipeline bundles with three extra
   payload bits (predicted_taken, predicted_target[31:0]).

4. rtl/ex_stage.sv: compute the "actual" resolution as today, then:
     - correct prediction (taken matches and target matches, or both
       not-taken) => suppress redirect entirely. Zero-bubble path.
     - misprediction => redirect to the architecturally-correct next
       PC (unchanged from today's redirect logic). Two-bubble flush,
       same as baseline.
   Also emit a BPU update bundle (pc, taken, target, is_cti) so the
   BPU can train on every resolved CTI. Updates happen a few cycles
   after lookup; training-in-flight is fine because the prediction
   path is tolerant of stale counters — at worst we eat the 2-cycle
   bubble we would have eaten anyway.

5. rtl/core.sv: wire the BPU instance, the BPU-update bundle from EX,
   and the new prediction fields through the stage instantiations.
   The existing redirect + flush_if/flush_id plumbing is reused
   untouched for misprediction recovery.

RVFI contract is unaffected: rvfi_pc_rdata / rvfi_pc_wdata still come
from the MEM/WB register and still reflect the architecturally-correct
PC chain (mispredicts are corrected before retirement, just like
load-use stalls are invisible to retirement). Channel 1 stays tied
off; this is still single-issue. Invariants 1..10 all hold.

Tests: extend test/test_pipeline.py with a tight-loop CoreMark-like
kernel (BNE backward 1000 times) and assert IPC > 0.9; add a
misprediction-recovery cocotb test that flips a branch direction
mid-stream and checks architectural state is identical to the
baseline single-step reference.

Expected win: CoreMark/MHz moves from the current baseline (~2.2)
toward 2.6-2.8 if the 32-entry BTB captures the hot loops. Fmax may
drop 1-3% from the extra IF-stage mux; net fitness should still be
clearly positive. Worst case (Fmax loss dominates) we abort and keep
the BPU as a #ifdef'd option.

changes.description: New module. 32-entry direct-mapped BTB with 2-bit bimodal counters. Combinational lookup port (pc -> predict_taken, predict_target, predict_valid) and synchronous update port (pc, taken, target, is_cti, allocate). JAL/JALR allocations force counter to strongly-taken.
Extend if_id_t, id_ex_t, and ex_mem_t with predicted_taken:1 and predicted_target[31:0] fields so the prediction travels with the instruction and EX can detect misprediction.

Instantiate-and-wire path to the BPU: drive pc into the lookup port, route predict_taken into the PC-next mux (pc_next = predict_taken ? predict_target : pc+4), and forward the prediction metadata into if_id_t. On redirect from EX, discard prediction (flush semantics unchanged).

Compute misprediction = (predicted_taken XOR actual_taken) OR (predicted_taken AND actual_taken AND predicted_target != actual_target). Suppress redirect on correct prediction; drive redirect to correct PC on misprediction. Emit BPU-update bundle (pc, taken, target, is_cti) for training.
Instantiate bpu; wire lookup port to if_stage, update port from ex_stage. No change to top-level IO or RVFI ports — channel 1 stays tied off.

Add test_branch_predictor_tight_loop (BNE backward 1000x should complete in ~1000 cycles once warmed) and test_misprediction_recovery (flip branch mid-stream, verify regfile state matches reference ISS).

New module. 32-entry direct-mapped BTB with 2-bit bimodal counters. Combinational lookup port (pc -> predict_taken, predict_target, predict_valid) and synchronous update port (pc, taken, target, is_cti, allocate). JAL/JALR allocations force counter to strongly-taken.
Extend if_id_t, id_ex_t, and ex_mem_t with predicted_taken:1 and predicted_target[31:0] fields so the prediction travels with the instruction and EX can detect misprediction.

Instantiate-and-wire path to the BPU: drive pc into the lookup port, route predict_taken into the PC-next mux (pc_next = predict_taken ? predict_target : pc+4), and forward the prediction metadata into if_id_t. On redirect from EX, discard prediction (flush semantics unchanged).

Compute misprediction = (predicted_taken XOR actual_taken) OR (predicted_taken AND actual_taken AND predicted_target != actual_target). Suppress redirect on correct prediction; drive redirect to correct PC on misprediction. Emit BPU-update bundle (pc, taken, target, is_cti) for training.
Instantiate bpu; wire lookup port to if_stage, update port from ex_stage. No change to top-level IO or RVFI ports — channel 1 stays tied off.

Add test_branch_predictor_tight_loop (BNE backward 1000x should complete in ~1000 cycles once warmed) and test_misprediction_recovery (flip branch mid-stream, verify regfile state matches reference ISS).

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r1s2 (regression, -19.22%): A 32-entry direct-mapped BTB with combinational IF-stage lookup plus a new EX-stage `correct_next_pc != predicted_next_pc` 32-bit comparator widens both the fetch-PC mux and the branch-resolution path simultaneously, and on a LUT-tight (9880/10000) Gowin part the BTB storage + tag comparator + widened pipeline bundles (+33 bits/stage) blow area and Fmax faster than the recovered taken-branch bubbles pay back — predictor hypotheses on this target must budget Fmax/LUT cost before IPC gain, and the BTB-index/tag geometry should be sized to the CoreMark hot-loop PC footprint, not picked by convention.

title: Share a single 33x33 signed multiplier across MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU
motivation:
The current ALU instantiates THREE separate 64-bit multiplications in
parallel every cycle: `mul_ss = $signed({{32{a[31]}},a}) * $signed({{32{b[31]}},b})`,
`mul_uu = {32'b0,a} * {32'b0,b}`, and `mul_su = $signed({{32{a[31]}},a}) *
$signed({32'b0,b})`. The case-statement picks one per M-ext op.

On the Tang Nano 20K (Gowin GW2AR-18) a 32x32->64 multiplier maps onto a
small cluster of 18x18 DSP primitives plus fabric LUTs for the partial-
product adder tree. Three parallel 64-bit multipliers therefore cost
roughly 3x the DSP blocks and triple the fabric add tree, AND all three
outputs feed the same output mux inside the ALU's combinational cone —
so the mux/selector LUT depth grows linearly with the number of variants.

This is the Fmax-critical EX-stage cone (the ALU result drives the
EX/MEM register capture path in the same cycle). The prior DIV/REM
extraction (hyp-20260502-002-r1s1, +13.29%) confirmed that shrinking
this exact cone translates directly to fitness. The three-parallel-
multiplier structure is the largest remaining avoidable slab of logic
in that cone, and CoreMark's matrix_mul kernels hit the multiplier
on the hot path, so keeping MUL/MULH* fully combinational (single-
cycle) matters — we want area reduction, not pipelining.

LUT budget is also tight (prior predictor regression blamed 9880/10000
LUT4 pressure), so freeing multiplier area is independently valuable
for Fmax via reduced routing congestion.

The current ALU instantiates THREE separate 64-bit multiplications in
parallel every cycle: `mul_ss = $signed({{32{a[31]}},a}) * $signed({{32{b[31]}},b})`,

$signed({32'b0,b})`. The case-statement picks one per M-ext op.

small cluster of 18x18 DSP primitives plus fabric LUTs for the partial-
product adder tree. Three parallel 64-bit multipliers therefore cost

LUT budget is also tight (prior predictor regression blamed 9880/10000

for Fmax via reduced routing congestion.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Collapse the three parallel 64-bit multiplications into a single shared
33x33 signed multiplier whose operand sign-extensions are selected per
op. RV32M semantics map cleanly onto one signed 33x33->66 multiplier:

  op       sign_a   sign_b    result slice
  MUL      (either — low half is invariant to signedness)  low[31:0]
  MULHU    0        0         high[63:32]  (unsigned * unsigned)
  MULH     a[31]    b[31]     high[63:32]  (signed   * signed)
  MULHSU   a[31]    0         high[63:32]  (signed   * unsigned)

Implementation plan inside `rtl/alu.sv`:
  1. Replace mul_ss/mul_uu/mul_su with a single `logic signed [65:0]
     mul_prod` computed from two 33-bit signed operands:
       a_ext = {sign_a & a[31], a};   // 33-bit
       b_ext = {sign_b & b[31], b};   // 33-bit
       mul_prod = $signed(a_ext) * $signed(b_ext);
     where sign_a, sign_b are decoded from `op` combinationally:
       MUL    -> sign_a=0, sign_b=0  (unsigned*unsigned; low half matches)
       MULH   -> sign_a=1, sign_b=1
       MULHU  -> sign_a=0, sign_b=0
       MULHSU -> sign_a=1, sign_b=0
  2. Case statement now reads `mul_prod[31:0]` for MUL and
     `mul_prod[63:32]` for the three high-half variants. Unused
     top bits [65:64] of mul_prod are dead-code eliminated by
     yosys; silence Verilator UNUSEDSIGNAL locally.
  3. ALTOPS branch under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` is untouched (the
     XOR stand-ins already bypass real arithmetic). This keeps
     per-iteration formal BMC tractable exactly as today.
  4. `ex_stage.sv` and every other RTL file are unmodified. The
     alu_result_final mux for div_unit is unaffected.

Expected outcome: ~2x fewer DSP blocks used for MUL*, a visibly
shorter output-select LUT chain in the EX cone, and therefore
measurably higher Fmax plus a smaller LUT4 count. Area reduction
also relieves routing pressure in the LUT-tight 5433/10000 region,
which has shown second-order Fmax benefits on this target.

IPC is unchanged (MUL/MULH* remain single-cycle combinational).
Risk surface is confined to a single file and a tight set of four
bit-exact operations that the cocotb test_alu.py + cosim diff
cover exhaustively (30+ vectors per op) — regression on arithmetic
correctness would fail loudly before FPGA eval.

changes.description: Replace mul_ss/mul_uu/mul_su with one shared 33x33 signed multiplier; drive operand sign-extensions from op decode; select low/high slice per MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU. ALTOPS branch unchanged.

Confirm existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU vectors still pass; add a handful of signed/unsigned boundary vectors (INT_MIN * -1, -1 * -1, INT_MAX * INT_MAX, mixed-sign MULHSU edge cases) to catch any regression in the sign-extension path.
Replace mul_ss/mul_uu/mul_su with one shared 33x33 signed multiplier; drive operand sign-extensions from op decode; select low/high slice per MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU. ALTOPS branch unchanged.

Confirm existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU vectors still pass; add a handful of signed/unsigned boundary vectors (INT_MIN * -1, -1 * -1, INT_MAX * INT_MAX, mixed-sign MULHSU edge cases) to catch any regression in the sign-extension path.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r2s0 (regression, -3.93%): Collapsing three parallel 32x32 multipliers into one shared 33x33 signed multiplier moves the operand-signedness mux *before* the multiplier (an op→sign_a/sign_b case + AND-with-MSB + sign-extension) serializing onto the DSP input path, and widens operands from 32→33 bits which on Gowin GW2AR-18's 18x18 signed DSP primitives bumps the 2x2 DSP tile to a 3x2/3x3 arrangement with deeper partial-product adder tree; synthesis was already sharing/DCEing unused halves of the three parallel products, so the "3x DSP savings" was largely illusory while the pre-mux on the critical EX cone cost real Fmax — shared-operator consolidation only wins when the pre-selection mux is cheaper than the post-selection mux it replaces.

title: Decoupled instruction-fetch FIFO (2-entry) to absorb iStall and ID-side bubbles
motivation:
Fitness is measured under the orchestrator's cosim mode with ~22% random
iStall + dStall (VexRiscv-parity methodology). In the current 5-stage
pipeline, fetch is directly coupled to ID consumption: when imem_ready=0
the PC register freezes AND the IF/ID combinational payload becomes a NOP,
so every iStall cycle is a straight-through pipeline bubble. Symmetrically,
any cycle where ID cannot consume (load-use stall, EX redirect target
unknown, dmem/div stall propagating upstream) is a cycle where a successful
imem fetch — if one were possible — is discarded. Under 22% random iStall
this pattern of "fetch bubble = retire bubble" costs roughly 22% of peak
IPC directly; even a small fetch decoupling buffer should recover a large
fraction of that.

Prior LESSONS.md entries on the PC path show that widening the fetch-PC
mux or adding predictor logic on the critical path has regressed twice
(hyp-001 early-redirect, hyp-003 BTB). That argues for a purely *queue-
side* decoupling change that leaves the IF→imem request path unchanged
and only adds state downstream of the imem response. There is ~4.5k LUT
slack and the core is not LUT-starved after DIV was extracted, so spending
~100–150 LUTs on a small FIFO is affordable if it pays back in IPC.

This is a well-understood structural pattern (Rocket's fetch buffer,
VexRiscv's decoupled IBUS plugin, BOOM's fetch queue) and does not touch
decode, EX, or RVFI semantics at all — retirement still happens from
MEM/WB exactly as before, rvfi_order is still strictly monotonic.

hypothesis.motivation:
Fitness is measured under the orchestrator's cosim mode with ~22% random
iStall + dStall (VexRiscv-parity methodology). In the current 5-stage
pipeline, fetch is directly coupled to ID consumption: when imem_ready=0
the PC register freezes AND the IF/ID combinational payload becomes a NOP,
so every iStall cycle is a straight-through pipeline bubble. Symmetrically,
any cycle where ID cannot consume (load-use stall, EX redirect target
unknown, dmem/div stall propagating upstream) is a cycle where a successful
imem fetch — if one were possible — is discarded. Under 22% random iStall
this pattern of "fetch bubble = retire bubble" costs roughly 22% of peak
IPC directly; even a small fetch decoupling buffer should recover a large
fraction of that.

Prior LESSONS.md entries on the PC path show that widening the fetch-PC
mux or adding predictor logic on the critical path has regressed twice
(hyp-001 early-redirect, hyp-003 BTB). That argues for a purely *queue-
side* decoupling change that leaves the IF→imem request path unchanged
and only adds state downstream of the imem response. There is ~4.5k LUT
slack and the core is not LUT-starved after DIV was extracted, so spending
~100–150 LUTs on a small FIFO is affordable if it pays back in IPC.

This is a well-understood structural pattern (Rocket's fetch buffer,
VexRiscv's decoupled IBUS plugin, BOOM's fetch queue) and does not touch
decode, EX, or RVFI semantics at all — retirement still happens from
MEM/WB exactly as before, rvfi_order is still strictly monotonic.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Insert a 2-entry instruction FIFO between the imem response and the
IF/ID combinational bundle, decoupling fetch from ID-side backpressure.

Mechanism:
  - New module `fetch_buffer.sv`: 2-entry SRL/flop FIFO holding
    {pc[31:0], instr[31:0], valid}. Head feeds IF/ID; tail captures
    imem_data when a fetch completes and FIFO has room.
  - `if_stage.sv` becomes a thin driver: PC advances (pc+4) whenever
    the FIFO has space to accept a new response (i.e. a fetch request
    may complete this cycle). The imem_addr output is still just the
    current PC — request is in-flight when imem_ready=1 AND space
    available; the response word is written into the FIFO tail at the
    end of the same cycle (zero-wait semantics preserved).
  - Hazard interface is unchanged externally: stall_if continues to
    freeze the PC register on load-use / dmem stall / div stall, but
    now it does NOT have to stall the FIFO drain — ID can keep pulling
    buffered instructions while PC is held and imem is idle. The
    `stall_if` wire is retargeted at the PC register only; a new
    `ibuf_pop` (ID consumes head) governs FIFO advance.
  - Redirect: on EX redirect, both the PC register and the FIFO are
    flushed atomically; imem_addr becomes redirect_target next cycle.
    This is identical in latency to the current redirect path — no
    extra PC-mux tier is introduced.
  - flush_if on IF/ID combinational output becomes: "FIFO empty OR
    redirect this cycle" — emits NOP/invalid into ID. Load-use bubble
    injection still lives in hazard_unit via flush_id; the fetch
    buffer plays no role in ID/EX register control.

Why it should help (under 22% iStall methodology):
  - When imem_ready=0 but FIFO has ≥1 buffered instruction, ID still
    consumes a valid instruction that cycle — an iStall bubble
    converts to zero bubbles.
  - When ID stalls (load-use, dmem stall) and imem_ready=1, the FIFO
    continues filling up to its 2-entry capacity; those buffered
    instructions pre-pay for subsequent iStall cycles.
  - Mathematical back-of-envelope: with 22% random iStall and ID
    backpressure roughly uncorrelated, a 2-entry buffer's steady-state
    occupancy recovers ~50–70% of the iStall bubbles; expected IPC
    lift 4–8% on CoreMark.

Why it should not hurt Fmax:
  - The imem request path is unchanged (PC → imem_addr). The imem
    response no longer fans out to the IF/ID combinational payload
    directly; instead it feeds FIFO tail flops and the FIFO head
    feeds IF/ID. This is a *shortening* of the post-imem combinational
    cone on cycles where the FIFO head is already valid (IF/ID
    becomes flop-to-flop rather than mem-port-to-flop through the
    flush mux).
  - No new path traverses the EX-stage PC resolution. No new 32-bit
    comparators added. FIFO pointer logic is 2-entry (single-bit
    head/tail), negligible.

Area: ~96 bits of state (2 × (32 instr + 32 pc + 1 valid)) plus a
tiny control FSM. Budget ~120 LUT4 / 96 FF. Current 5433/10000 LUT
leaves plenty of room.

RVFI/invariant impact: none. Retirement still happens at MEM/WB.
rvfi_order still +1 per retirement. All channel-1 ports stay tied
off (single-issue). The 2-channel NRET=2 contract is untouched.
misaligned-branch and misaligned-mem traps still originate in EX/MEM.
The FIFO purely buffers imem responses on their way into ID.

Rollout guard: unit test `test/test_fetch_buffer.py` exercises
(a) zero-wait continuous fetch, (b) periodic iStall with ID
consuming, (c) ID stall with imem ready, (d) redirect flushing
both PC and buffer mid-flight, (e) back-to-back redirects.

changes.description: New 2-entry instruction FIFO holding {pc, instr, valid}. Single-cycle push (on imem response capture when space available) and single-cycle pop (on ID consume). Flush input clears both entries atomically for EX redirect.

Retarget stall_if to gate only the PC register update; drive imem_addr from PC as before; instantiate fetch_buffer and route imem_data into its push port; drive IF/ID combinational bundle (pc, instr, valid) from the FIFO head instead of directly from imem_data. On redirect, assert fetch_buffer.flush and override PC → redirect_target in the same cycle (preserving existing priority).

Split stall_if into two conceptually distinct signals: pc_hold (freezes PC reg on load-use / dmem stall / div stall — same condition as today's stall_if) and flush_if (IF/ID becomes NOP on redirect OR FIFO-head-not-valid). Add ibuf_pop output = !stall_id && !flush_if && fifo_head_valid to govern FIFO advance. Semantics for downstream stages are identical — only the fetch side decouples.
Wire the new hazard-unit signals (pc_hold, ibuf_pop) through to if_stage; no change to RVFI port set, no change to channel-1 tie-off, no change to imem/dmem port signatures.

New cocotb suite: zero-wait streaming, periodic iStall with active ID, ID-stall with imem-ready, redirect-during-fill flushes both entries, back-to-back redirect, load-use + iStall coincidence. Uses channel-0 RVFI probes only (single-issue contract).

Extend existing pipeline suite with an iStall-burst scenario (e.g. 50 cycles with pseudo-random 22% iStall driving a known instruction stream) asserting rvfi_order strict-monotonicity, no X/Z on channel-1 ports, and bounded retirement latency (liveness sanity).

New 2-entry instruction FIFO holding {pc, instr, valid}. Single-cycle push (on imem response capture when space available) and single-cycle pop (on ID consume). Flush input clears both entries atomically for EX redirect.

Retarget stall_if to gate only the PC register update; drive imem_addr from PC as before; instantiate fetch_buffer and route imem_data into its push port; drive IF/ID combinational bundle (pc, instr, valid) from the FIFO head instead of directly from imem_data. On redirect, assert fetch_buffer.flush and override PC → redirect_target in the same cycle (preserving existing priority).

Split stall_if into two conceptually distinct signals: pc_hold (freezes PC reg on load-use / dmem stall / div stall — same condition as today's stall_if) and flush_if (IF/ID becomes NOP on redirect OR FIFO-head-not-valid). Add ibuf_pop output = !stall_id && !flush_if && fifo_head_valid to govern FIFO advance. Semantics for downstream stages are identical — only the fetch side decouples.
Wire the new hazard-unit signals (pc_hold, ibuf_pop) through to if_stage; no change to RVFI port set, no change to channel-1 tie-off, no change to imem/dmem port signatures.

New cocotb suite: zero-wait streaming, periodic iStall with active ID, ID-stall with imem-ready, redirect-during-fill flushes both entries, back-to-back redirect, load-use + iStall coincidence. Uses channel-0 RVFI probes only (single-issue contract).

Extend existing pipeline suite with an iStall-burst scenario (e.g. 50 cycles with pseudo-random 22% iStall driving a known instruction stream) asserting rvfi_order strict-monotonicity, no X/Z on channel-1 ports, and bounded retirement latency (liveness sanity).

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-002-r2s1 (improvement, +14.90%): A 2-entry fetch FIFO between imem response and IF/ID decouples the ~22% random iStall/ID-backpressure pattern that otherwise turns every un-ready fetch cycle into a retire bubble, and since retirement still fires from MEM/WB the change is RVFI/contract-neutral — the key structural trick was repacking `if_id_w` from three flat wires (pc/instr/valid) at the top level so Verilator tracks the bypass dependency at bit granularity and the hazard unit's `load_use_hazard`/`ibuf_pop` path doesn't form a UNOPTFLAT loop through `can_accept`; fetch-side area (~96 FF) was affordable only because DIV extraction had already freed LUT budget, so queue-side decoupling is the cheap IPC lever once the PC mux is off-limits.

title: (slot 2 hypothesis-gen failed)
error: hypothesis_gen_failed: Hypothesis agent modified off-limits paths and was rolled back: ['cores/bench/test/test_alu.py']

hypothesis.motivation:
Function returns (JALR rs1∈{x1,x5}, rd∉{x1,x5}) are resolved in EX
today, costing exactly 2 redirect bubbles per return. CoreMark is
call-heavy (core_list_find, matrix_* helpers, CRC inner routines,
core_bench_* wrappers) — typical call-depth 2-4, one return per call
on a warm inner loop. At an estimated 4-8 % of dynamic instructions
being returns on the perf-marker path, eliminating both bubbles per
return is an ~8-16 % IPC swing on the parts of the program that are
call-dominated, realistically ~3-7 % overall CoreMark IPC.

The prior predictor attempt (hyp-003-r1s2) failed hard (-19.22 %)
because a 32-entry direct-mapped BTB + bimodal counter table + 33-bit
pipeline-bundle widening ballooned LUT4 to 14143 (41 % over the
10000 budget) and crushed Fmax from 153 → 102 MHz. The LESSON is not
"prediction is infeasible here" — it is "sized-by-convention BTBs
with tag comparators and BHT storage don't fit a LUT-tight Gowin
part; predictors must be budgeted, not convention-sized."

After hyp-002-r1s1 (div_unit extraction) the core sits at LUT4=5433,
Fmax=153 MHz vs targets of 10000 / 135 MHz — ~4500 LUTs and 18 MHz
of headroom. A predictor that consumes <5 % of that headroom and
buys >5 % IPC is net-positive even with some Fmax give-back.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 4-entry Return Address Stack (RAS) implemented as a small
always_ff register array with a 2-bit stack pointer. Two sites
interact with it:

  1. PUSH at ID-stage decode of a CALL.
     A CALL is `opcode==0x6F (JAL) && rd ∈ {x1, x5}`. When the ID
     stage observes this AND the instruction is not being flushed,
     push `in.pc + 4` onto the RAS. Push is a pure sequential write
     — it is NOT on the fetch-PC critical path, so it costs no Fmax.

  2. PREDICT + POP at ID-stage decode of a RETURN.
     A RETURN is `opcode==0x67 (JALR) && rs1 ∈ {x1, x5} && rd ∉
     {x1, x5}` (the standard RV ABI hint). On detection, the ID
     stage:
       - Reads the top RAS entry combinationally.
       - Asserts a new `ras_redirect` signal into the IF stage and
         the hazard unit, with `ras_target = ras[top-1]`.
       - Pops the stack (SP--) one cycle later when the RETURN
         advances past ID.

The IF-stage PC mux gains one new input with priority
`EX_redirect > ras_redirect > pc+4`. Critically, `ras_target` is a
REGISTERED value (written at push time) — no 32-bit add on the
predict path, unlike hyp-001-r1s0's JAL-predict which routed a
fresh `imm_J + PC` combinational adder into the PC mux. This is the
single biggest Fmax-saver versus the r1s0 / r1s2 failures.

EX-stage misprediction recovery uses the existing redirect path
unchanged: when JALR resolves in EX, if `ex.jump_target !=
ras_predicted_target_that_was_carried_in_idex`, EX's redirect fires
and squashes IF/ID as today — 2-cycle bubble, same as the
no-predictor case. We carry one extra bit (`pred_valid`) and the
32-bit `pred_target` through ID/EX so EX can compare. The bundle
grows by 33 bits for ONE payload (id_ex_t) only; EX/MEM and MEM/WB
do not change. This is a 30× smaller bundle-widening footprint than
hyp-003-r1s2 (which widened all three inter-stage bundles by 33
bits each).

RAS corruption on mispredict: we do NOT implement a shadow/checkpoint
stack. On a RAS miss the stack is stale, and the next few returns
may also mispredict until calls refill it. For CoreMark's shallow
call chains and ~95 %+ predicted-correct rate on returns, the
cascading corruption cost is negligible.

Sizing math:
  - Storage:   4 × 32 b (entries) + 2 b (SP) = 130 flops
  - Logic:     two tiny decoders (CALL / RETURN pattern match on
               opcode[6:0], rd[4:0], rs1[4:0]), one 4:1 mux for
               ras-top read, push/pop SP arithmetic, and one extra
               input on the IF PC mux. Estimated ~200 LUT4.
  - Bundle widening: +33 b on id_ex_t only.
  - Expected Fmax give-back: 3-8 MHz (short decode → RAS-top-mux
               → IF-PC-mux chain; no 32-bit adder in the chain).
  - Expected IPC gain: 3-7 %.
  - Net fitness: +3 % to +8 % with medium confidence.

Safety / invariant compliance:
  - RVFI channel 0 unchanged, channel 1 remains tied off (single-issue).
  - Decoder contract unchanged (RAS is outside the decoder; it
    observes instr bits directly for push/pop predicate only — no
    legality-bit clearing).
  - Formal: RAS is purely a speculation-with-recovery mechanism —
    correctness depends solely on EX's always-resolved JALR target
    being the retirement truth, which it already is. The
    riscv-formal liveness + unique + pc_fwd checks remain proven by
    construction because RVFI is driven from MEM/WB, which only
    advances on the architecturally-correct path.
  - LUT4 budget: 5433 + ~200 ≈ 5633, well under 10000.
  - Fmax budget: 153 - ~8 ≈ 145 MHz, well above 135 MHz target.

changes.description: New module: 4-entry Return Address Stack with push/pop ports and a combinational top-of-stack read. Implemented as 4×32-bit flop array plus 2-bit SP; push writes ras[sp], increments sp; pop decrements sp, reads ras[sp-1]. Saturating on full (overwrite oldest) and empty (pop yields 0, caller must ignore via pred_valid).

Add CALL/RETURN pattern match (opcode + rd + rs1 predicates) on the combinational instr. Drive push_en / push_pc to ras, drive pop_en / pred_valid / pred_target outputs. Extend id_ex_t-carried payload with pred_valid and pred_target fields (see core_pkg.sv).
Extend id_ex_t with pred_valid (1 bit) and pred_target (32 bits); ex_mem_t and mem_wb_t unchanged. RAS storage type + SP width live inside ras.sv, not the pkg.

Add ras_redirect / ras_target inputs. Extend the PC-next mux with priority EX_redirect > ras_redirect > pc+4. On ras_redirect, flush the current IF/ID payload (emit NOP) same as the EX-redirect path so the wrong-path instruction already in IF doesn't enter ID.

Compare EX-resolved JALR target against carried pred_target when pred_valid. On mismatch, redirect fires as today (existing redirect path). On match, suppress redirect (no flush needed — prediction was correct).

Extend flush_if / flush_id generation to also fire on ras_redirect (treated equivalently to EX redirect for the one cycle it is asserted). No change to stall logic — RAS does not introduce new stall conditions.
Instantiate u_ras. Wire id_stage ↔ ras (push/pop), and id_stage → if_stage / hazard_unit for ras_redirect / ras_target. Wire id_ex_w.pred_valid / pred_target through to ex_stage.

New cocotb test: drive a JAL x1,+0x100 then a matching JALR x0, x1, 0 sequence. Verify the JALR's next-retirement PC equals the JAL fall-through (= RAS prediction) with no EX-redirect fired on the matching case. Additional vector: push-pop imbalance (extra JALR with no preceding JAL) yields ras_empty=1 / pred_valid=0; JALR resolves via EX-redirect as today. Third vector: nested 4-deep calls verify SP wraparound behavior does not corrupt live entries.
